item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be found under any source as cc-compliant. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well dude, read the date.
It's hard to make changes to such an old article.

Downvote it if you want, but it ain't gonna change much.
What about using this kind of attention on new articles rather than trying to change what the site was back almost 10 fucking years ago?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a generic magic item, and an overpowered one at that. It also breaks the Desirable Item Rule and its execution is not good enough to do anything.

Also if its SCP are litterally "put it in a box". Safe by definition.

item.title: Re: Test log clarification
item.content:encoded: The bacteria thrive on their own but the water also boosts the immune system of the human drinker, overwhelming the increases bacteria growth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But an explanation is right there in the article:

Addendum 109-1: Upon the item's delivery to Site 19, it was given the object class of Safe. As tests were conducted on the item, uncertainty surrounding test results prompted Gen. ████████ ████ to upgrade the object class to Euclid.

Take this test for example:

TEST 4: Streptococcus bacteria cultured in water from SCP-109 thrived and multiplied quickly. Water from SCP-109 administered to subject suffering from streptococcal infection killed nearly all bacteria and produced a full recovery within 24 hours.

So, this canteen first produced water beneficial for the bacteria, and then for a human. Does it decide who to help?

Then it might be sentient, which promotes it to Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would like to see a more detailed explanation of why this is classified as Euclid. As currently written, this item seems extremely Safe, even beneficial.

item.title: Test log clarification
item.content:encoded: In Test 4, the log says initially that 109's contents caused streptococcus to thrive, but then in the next sentence it says that it killed all streptococcus and cured those infected with it when ingested. Which did you mean? Did you mean it to be like this, or…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this SCP. I really do. We need some SCP'S that are mostly just useful, and this would be quite the helpful item in an indefinite trip.

There's just one problem. Addendum 109-3 seems like Lolfoundation. Telling D-class to completely chug a bottomless canteen. And this was addressed to more than a few people. Can we get rid of that and nothing else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pour some of this magic water in the turtle shell!
It's a magic item. The healthy water is far from unique enough to save it, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is good for the health of living things, but prioritizes more complex organisms, or those with higher intelligence or sentience or whatever?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really want to +1 this but I don't see why it's euclid. It wont pose much threat unless you leave it pouring out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know what, I can accept this in a weird little pseudocanon where universe is not constantly trying to kill us and destroy itself, and thus lolFoundation isn't as bad.

I'll leave it as +-0, but it still was a fun read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Instead of a Klein Bottle, we have Dr. Klein's Canteen. Very subtle.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I want to see this one cross tested with 914

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What if you just keep pouring it? Or cut it open?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not gonna lie, even with test 4, I think this should be safe. Increasing the rate of bacterial growth doesn't impact containment since containment would need to be breached for that to happen. This is simple as hell to contain, and poses little to no risk if people don't leave it tipped over with the cap removed, and the former is all that is needed to be considered safe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it makes bacteria stronger if they're cultured in it, but kills them if they've infected a host? That doesn't make much sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When the SCP you are reading makes you want to have a drink of water from it.
+1'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rate of flow, please? Theories as to the point of origin of the water? What does it do if the pressure outside is greater than that inside? Can water be forced in; can it act as an infinite reservoir?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, my best guess is Test 4:

TEST 4: Streptococcus bacteria cultured in water from SCP-109 thrived and multiplied quickly. Water from SCP-109 administered to subject suffering from streptococcal infection killed nearly all bacteria and produced a full recovery within 24 hours.

Specifically, the fact that bacteria thrived and multiplied quickly, though this doesn't actually explain how quickly it was compared to normal growth. Potentially, possibly, maybe it could be used in the development of bioweapons to grow bacteria at a rapid rate.

item.title: Decanter of Endless Water
item.content:encoded:
Anyone? I'm surprised someone else hasn't made the comparison to the D&D item. Always try to get one in any campaign because with that, you can tell it how fast to pour, so we always have a stronger party member shout "geyser!" and hose down smaller enemies that can't withstand the blast. Knock 'em down for attacks of opportunity and such. lol

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It doesn't say it explicitly in the article, but I imagine it's a sort of long-term experiment to see what sort of effect the water might have on the plant over a really long time. Like, we know it has mild healing properties with people… does it make the plant sturdier than another plant of the same type being watered with "regular" water?

Maybe a slight edit in that regard would be helpful in clarifying that… though I certainly won't make the edit myself without some sort of approval/help with wording it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like it. It's just an infinite supply of magic water. And why is it Euclid? Am I missing something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, it's simple and straight forward and weird, if not a bit predicable. I had half expected some dark twist on it healing people too well, or making bacteria and other organisms too strong.

I do have to question them letting someone use it to water their plant. Surely there are better things to be using it for than that? Right? And easier ways to get a cup of water to pour on some desk plant..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: a few experiments I would want to try, copying it scp-038, sustaining an entire facility off of it for an indefinite period. If both of those things work adding a copy of it at each facility and several at site 62 would be very useful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel tempted to +1 for "Dr Klein" though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is too lolFoundation by modern standards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So it's an infinite supply of healing water? Doesn't that make it a good thing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"It has come to my attention that new class D personnel are often dared to empty the bottle. Guards are reminded that they are to discourage such activity, and inform them that SCP-109 is bottomless. Chanting 'Chug!' repeatedly is considered unprofessional." — Dr. Klein

:|

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Slowest flood ever. It is to my understanding that, while it has unlimited water, it still only exits the canteen at a normal rate.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yea,imagine if somebody took it to the top of mount Everest and just left it open and laying sideways,hello biblical scale flood.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It does seem potentially very dangerous. If it were knocked over, it'd just keep flooding the area until it were uprighted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I assume it was based on the older definition of Euclid; I don't see why it would be under the current guidelines.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get why this is Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mentioning Klein and an endless water container in the same sentence… I see what you did there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing special, but we need some mundane SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "CHUG! CHUG! CHUG! CHUG! CHUG! CHUG! CHUG! CHUG! CHUG! CHUG! CHUG! giving up already?" this mental image just wont go away. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it's my understanding that SCP-109 is a normal canteen, except for the fact that it is bottomless.

1. Water balloon test - Because SCP-109 has no means of water propulsion other than gravity, it cannot fill a water balloon. I would also like to remind all personnel that

2. Electronic test - Other than the slight metal content of SCP-109, it acts as normal water and the results are the same with water from a normal tap.

3. Dumping in polluted river - Test parameters are unclear, dumping the water into a polluted river will dilute it with sufficient time, dumping the whole of SCP-109, with the means to retrieve it, will dilute the river and will cause the river level to rise until SCP-109 is retrieved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So what happens if you try filling a water balloon with this thing? Would the balloon pop at some point?

What happens if you dump it on something electronic?
What happens if you dump it in a polluted river?
